reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Core mission to reduce segregation and ensure equitable access to quality education for disadvantaged and Roma pupils.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils; those currently locked out of selective tracks.
reactions.public_good_frame: Fair allocation of educational opportunity based on potential, not family wealth or test-prep access; prevention of early sorting that compounds SES-linked advantage.
reactions.argument: Quotas mechanically increase disadvantaged representation IF a workable SES indicator exists and IF low-SES families apply at similar rates to high-SES families. But the scenario itself flags critical unresolved premises: no Hungarian student-level SES register exists; rural low-SES application rates are unknown; and private/church school evasion could absorb displaced demand. Worse, if quotas select the least-prepared among the prepared (poorest families with tutoring access), the deepest excluded—families with no advocacy and no information—remain outside regardless. S1 improves intake composition IF these assumptions hold, but without proof of SES-indicator validity and rural participation data, it risks legitimising quotas as adequate while the most excluded stay excluded.
reactions.condition_to_change: Evidence that: (1) the proposed SES indicator is technically valid and administratively workable at application time; (2) rural and Roma family application rates rise to match urban rates; (3) private/church enrollment does not absorb displaced demand; (4) intake SES composition shifts toward lower-SES pupils, not toward 'slightly less high-SES' pupils.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Core mission to reduce segregation and ensure equitable access to quality education for disadvantaged and Roma pupils.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils; families currently unable to compete for selective-track entry.
reactions.public_good_frame: Genuine mixed peer environments in common schools that provide intellectual challenge and access to high-achieving peers for all pupils, not concentrated disadvantage in one track and advantage in another.
reactions.argument: Phasing down selective entry is structurally sound IF it reduces sorting. But the scenario identifies its own critical risk: demand migrates to unregulated non-state schools. If middle-class families exit to church/private tracks and poorest families lack money/information to follow, the result is worse segregation for the most vulnerable—concentrated in underfunded general-school cohorts with no high-achieving peers. General-school capacity to challenge high-achievers is unproven in Hungary. S2 only helps if general schools are credibly funded at parity, non-state evasion is prevented, and the 'largest risk' the scenario identifies does not materialise.
reactions.condition_to_change: Evidence that: (1) non-state enrollment is prevented or strongly capped, not merely hoped to stabilize; (2) general schools receive per-student funding parity with former selective tracks; (3) pilot data shows general schools retain high-achievers without middle-class flight in first transition cohorts; (4) no widening of SES-gradient within general-school cohorts as reform rolls out.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Core mission to reduce segregation and ensure equitable access to quality education for disadvantaged and Roma pupils.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils; families at highest risk of concentration in under-resourced, low-peer-quality schools.
reactions.public_good_frame: Structural elimination of the exclusion mechanism itself, not relocation of it to harder-to-monitor channels (school choice, non-state schools) where harm concentrates on the most vulnerable.
reactions.argument: S3 removes the formal selection point, but identifies its own largest risk with clarity: sorting migrates to school choice and non-state maintainers, leaving poorest families—least mobile, least informed, unable to afford non-state fees—concentrated in public comprehensive schools with worst peer composition and fewest resources. This is structurally worse for the most disadvantaged than today. The Finnish analogy is misleading: Finland executed tracking removal bundled with universal teacher retraining, national curriculum standardization, and zero private alternative. Hungary's scenario offers none of that. Without simultaneous prevention of school-choice sorting AND binding comprehensive funding/staffing standards AND credible entrenchment against private evasion, this reform concentrates harm on the children it claims to help.
reactions.condition_to_change: Supportable only if: (1) legislative entrenchment prevents school-choice and non-state sorting by law or subsidy regulation, not merely hopes to avoid it; (2) comprehensive schools receive legally-protected funding guarantees and teacher-training standards proven sufficient for mixed-ability cohorts (Hungarian data, not Finnish analogy); (3) pilot monitoring shows no widening between-school SES gradient and no increase in household segregation; (4) genuine fall-back plan exists if cross-party pact fails, so families are not abandoned mid-transition.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Core mission to reduce segregation and ensure equitable access to quality education for disadvantaged and Roma pupils.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils; families for whom structural exclusion is the root problem, not insufficient funding of the second-tier track.
reactions.public_good_frame: Genuine equity through removal of structural mechanisms of exclusion, not perpetuation of unequal structure with incremental resource transfers reversible in budget cycles.
reactions.argument: S4 treats the symptom (under-resourced general schools) not the disease (age-10/12 early selection that sorts families into permanent legal tracks). Compensation is politically fragile: the scenario's own political-risks section concedes these are the first lines cut in fiscal consolidation. Worse, making the unequal structure more tolerable through compensation risks legitimising it as permanent, foreclosing political space for structural reform (S1/S2/S3) that achieves equity. The scenario admits compensation cannot offset selection effects at Hungary's selection age. For the most vulnerable, this offers: slightly better resources for a few budget cycles, then cuts, then they are worse-than-baseline because reform energy has been spent on managed inequality, not structural change.
reactions.condition_to_change: No condition makes S4 acceptable as standalone reform, because perpetuating legal structural inequality—even with resource transfers—contradicts the interest in equitable access. S4 could be supported as a parallel measure while pursuing structural reform (e.g., compensation + quota reform, or compensation + phased phase-down), but only if the structural component is binding and durable. As a substitute for structural reform, it legitimises the architecture of exclusion itself.
